title: SaaS Unit Economics (LTV/CAC) Master Calculator

seo.title: SaaS LTV/CAC Calculator | Unit Economics Analysis Tool
seo.description: Professional SaaS Unit Economics calculator. Calculate Churn, MRR, LTV, CAC, and LTV/CAC ratios. Get AI-driven growth insights for your startup.

aiContext: This tool calculates customer acquisition cost (CAC), lifetime value (LTV), and churn. A ratio below 3:1 indicates growth inefficiency, while above 5:1 might suggest under-spending on marketing. High churn is a leaky bucket warning.

diagnostics.message: Efficiency Warning: Your LTV/CAC ratio is below the 3.0 benchmark. This suggests your acquisition costs are too high relative to customer value. Consider optimizing your ad channels or improving onboarding retention.
High Growth Signal: Your unit economics are exceptionally strong (LTV/CAC > 5.0). You have significant room to increase your sales and marketing spend to capture market share faster.
Cash Flow Alert: It takes more than 12 months to recoup your acquisition costs. This can strain cash flow. Look for ways to increase upfront payments or reduce onboarding time.
Leaky Bucket Warning: Monthly churn over 5% will severely limit your growth ceiling. Focus on customer success and product-market fit before scaling acquisition.
Healthy Economics: Your business is operating within the 'Golden Zone' of SaaS efficiency. Maintain this balance while scaling.

sources.name: Institutional GAAP standards for Revenue Recognition

article.heading: Understanding SaaS Unit Economics
article.body: Unit economics are the bedrock of any sustainable SaaS business. While MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue) tells you how big you are today, unit economics tell you how profitable you will be tomorrow. The two pillars of this analysis are LTV (Life Time Value) and CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost).

article.heading: The Golden Ratio: 3:1
article.body: In the venture capital world, an LTV/CAC ratio of 3:1 is considered the benchmark for a healthy SaaS company. If your ratio is lower, you are spending too much to acquire customers relative to what they are worth. If it is significantly higher (e.g., 8:1), you may actually be under-investing in growth and leaving market share on the table.

article.heading: The Hidden Killer: Churn Rate
article.body: Churn is the denominator in the LTV equation. Even a small increase in monthly churn can drastically reduce the lifetime value of a customer. For example, a 5% churn rate means the average customer stays for 20 months, while a 2% churn rate extends that to 50 months. Managing the 'leaky bucket' is often more cost-effective than acquiring new users.

article.heading: CAC Payback Period
article.body: This indicates how quickly you recoup your marketing spend. Top-performing SaaS companies aim for a payback period of under 12 months. This is critical for cash flow management, especially for bootstrapped startups.

faq.q: What is a good LTV/CAC ratio for Series A?
faq.a: Investors typically look for a ratio of 3.0 or higher. A ratio of 5.0+ is considered exceptional.

faq.q: Should I include salaries in CAC?
faq.a: Yes, 'fully loaded' CAC should include the salaries of sales and marketing teams, software tools, and ad spend.
